From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

(Redirected from [Società Anonima Stefano Pittaluga](/w/index.php?title=Societ%C3%A0_Anonima_Stefano_Pittaluga&redirect=no "Società Anonima Stefano Pittaluga"))

Italian film producer

| Stefano Pittaluga | |
| --- | --- |
| Stefano Pittaluga | |
| Born | 2 February 1887  [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa"), [Liguria](/wiki/Liguria "Liguria")   [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") |
| Died | 26 April 1932(1932-04-26) (aged 45)  [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), [Lazio](/wiki/Lazio "Lazio")   Italy |
| Occupation | Producer |
| Years active | 1924−1932 |

**Stefano Pittaluga** (2 February 1887 – 26 April 1932) was an Italian [film producer](/wiki/Film_producer "Film producer"), one of several figures who helped revive [Italian film production](/wiki/Cinema_of_Italy "Cinema of Italy") in the late 1920s and early 1930s.

Originally a [Genoa](/wiki/Genoa "Genoa")-based owner of [cinemas](/wiki/Movie_theatre "Movie theatre"), Pittaluga began to expand his business after 1924. He gained enormous power in the [distribution](/wiki/Film_distribution "Film distribution") sector by securing the rights to release the films of the [Hollywood](/wiki/Cinema_of_the_United_States "Cinema of the United States") companies [Warner Brothers](/wiki/Warner_Brothers "Warner Brothers"), [Universal Pictures](/wiki/Universal_Pictures "Universal Pictures") and [First National](/wiki/First_National_Pictures "First National Pictures"). Pittaluga also moved into the film production business around the same time, acquiring studios in [Turin](/wiki/Turin "Turin"). Pittaluga was able to re-invest the profits he made from releasing foreign films into domestic production. He produced a series of films featuring pulp [Strongman](/wiki/Strongman_(strength_athlete) "Strongman (strength athlete)") characters including [Sansone](/w/index.php?title=Sansone_(character)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Sansone (character) (page does not exist)") and [Saetta](/w/index.php?title=Saetta&action=edit&redlink=1 "Saetta (page does not exist)") and particularly [Maciste](/wiki/Maciste "Maciste"). The films led to him being the most commercially successful Italian producer of the era.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Pittaluga's power grew in 1926 when he acquired the traditional leading production outfit [Cines](/wiki/Cines "Cines") from the conglomerate [Unione Cinematografica Italiana](/wiki/Unione_Cinematografica_Italiana "Unione Cinematografica Italiana"). Following on the heels of the development of [sound film](/wiki/Sound_film "Sound film") in America, Britain and Germany, Pittaluga had the [Cines Studios](/wiki/Cines_Studios "Cines Studios") in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") fitted for sound production. Cines produced the first Italian sound film *[The Song of Love](/wiki/The_Song_of_Love_(1930_film) "The Song of Love (1930 film)")* (1930), although another of the company's films *[Resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_(1931_Italian_film) "Resurrection (1931 Italian film)")* (1931) was actually made earlier but released later. Cines became the dominant force of the early sound era.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Under Pittaluga the company specialised in releasing musicals and comedies. Much of the companies' output, often remakes of German or Hungarian films, were later branded "[White Telephone](/wiki/White_Telephone "White Telephone")" films. After Pittaluga's sudden death in 1932 the company continued to release the same kind of populist films, and were only overtaken when the [Fascist](/wiki/Fascist "Fascist") government of [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") invested large sums of money in production and constructed the vast [Cinecittà](/wiki/Cinecitt%C3%A0 "Cinecittà") complex in Rome.
